text:
Conscription has been largely absent from European security systems over the last few decades, since the transition to all-volunteer forces in the 1970s. Increasing security challenges have, however, brought conscription back into discussion in many European countries. Recent conscription systems are not simply a carbon copy of traditional conscription; they have been formulated within a neoliberal context where citizens are expected to have as much individual freedom of choice as possible. The Conscription of Europe project explores the current state of conscription across six European countries: Estonia, Finland, Lithuania, Norway, Sweden and Switzerland. Four of the six countries have continuously maintained conscription over the past few decades, while Lithuania and Sweden have recently reintroduced conscription as a response to increased security concerns.

Through an in-depth analysis of the historical and political circumstances surrounding each instance, this paper pinpoints three criteria that constitute the modern conscription era and therefore enables the development of a typology of conscription that is grounded in empirical research: the extent of the conscription of young people from each cohort, the extent to which the conscripted participate voluntarily, and the modalities of female conscription. In total, this paper develops a conscription typology consisting of three distinct models. Conscription is thus shown to have evolved from a Cold War relic into a system consistent with postmodern values and the existing security structure of the European continent.
